text: VO2max sets the upper limit for aerobic metabolism in endurance performance, while lactate threshold determines the fraction of VO2max that can be sustained in events longer than approximately 3,000 m.
Running speed at lactate threshold is determined by the interaction of VO2max, blood lactate threshold, and running economy, and is generally similar to or slightly slower than marathon race pace.
A physiological model combining VO2max of 84 ml·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹, lactate threshold at 85% of VO2max, and exceptional running economy predicts a marathon time of 1:57:58.
specific_value: VO2max 84 ml·kg⁻¹·min⁻¹, LT at 85% VO2max, predicted 1:57:58

text: Lactate threshold expressed as a percentage of VO2max is a key determinant of sustainable pace in marathon running, with elite values around 85% of VO2max.

Combinations of VO2max, lactate threshold, and running economy values previously reported in elite runners produce modeled marathon times significantly faster than the world record (2:06:50 at time of publication), suggesting current physiological limits have not been reached.

Running economy interacts with VO2max and lactate threshold as an independent physiological factor that determines actual performance, meaning two runners with identical VO2max and lactate threshold can differ substantially in race pace due to economy differences.
